text: Three 8.5mm robotic trocars are positioned in the peri-umbilical region, right lateral mid-abdomen, and left lateral mid-abdomen. The sigmoid colon is visibly extending into the hernia but is easily reduced. An adhesion between the sigmoid colon and the hernia defect is observed and left intact. The inferior epigastric vessels are visible running lateral to the hernia.

The surgeon begins creating a subperitoneal flap at the medial umbilical ligament, extending it inferiorly and across the left rectus muscle. The flap is raised with areolar tissue left intact to ensure robustness. As dissection continues, the inferior epigastric vessels are exposed and preserved. The dissection extends laterally and medially to expose the hernia defect with a wide margin. The hernia is reduced, and associated fat is pulled out of the femoral hernia defect.

The surgeon dissects along the anterior aspect of the femoral vessels, ensuring the mesh can sit flat against the retroperitoneum. Fat along Cooper's ligament is carefully dissected to allow direct placement of the mesh on musculoskeletal structures, preventing it from floating into the hernia defect. Fat overlying the femoral vessels is dissected back to ensure the mesh sits inferiorly without rolling up, preventing hernia recurrence.
The surgeon performs medial dissection to expose the iliopubic tract, ensuring the mesh will sit and overlap muscle and tendon rather than fat.

The surgeon completes the dissection, revealing the iliopubic tract superiorly, Cooper's ligament inferiorly, and the femoral vessels and inferior epigastric vessels laterally. The Progrip mesh, folded in quarters, is introduced into the surgical field. The mesh is marked at its center and positioned over the hernia defect. The surgeon carefully manipulates the mesh to ensure it adheres to musculoskeletal structures, pulling away surrounding fat to prevent the mesh from floating into the hernia defect. The mesh is unfolded and positioned flat, both medially and laterally, ensuring it does not curl.

The surgeon places the mesh underneath the dissected fat, unfolding it first inferiorly, then superiorly, ensuring it sits flat against the pelvic contour. The mesh is pressed down at the sides and corners to prevent rolling, securing its position against musculoskeletal structures.

The surgeon ensures the mesh is flat and covers the hernia defect with a wide margin. The subperitoneal flap is closed using an absorbable V-lock suture, fully extraperitonealizing the mesh to prevent adhesions. Any holes in the flap are also closed with the V-lock suture, and the suture is locked in place by backing up on itself.
